idioms.definition: Dealing with a problem early prevents it from becoming much worse later; prompt action avoids bigger trouble
examples: Get that roof tile fixed now – a stitch in time saves nine.
She addressed the team conflict immediately, knowing that a stitch in time saves nine.
It's worth spending an hour on maintenance today; a stitch in time can save a lot of costly repairs.
idioms.usageTip: The full proverb is 'a stitch in time saves nine', but it is often shortened to just 'a stitch in time'. Used to justify taking quick preventative action.

idioms.definition: Used to say that something will happen at the appropriate moment; there is no need to rush
examples: 'When will I get my results?' 'All in good time – be patient.'
She didn't push for a promotion, believing everything would happen all in good time.
All in good time – we'll explain everything once everyone has arrived.
idioms.usageTip: Often used as a reassuring phrase when someone is being impatient. Can sound slightly dismissive if said in the wrong tone.

idioms.definition: Happening or existing before the period when someone was born or old enough to experience or remember something
examples: That TV show was before your time – it aired back in the 1970s.
The original version of this song was before my time, but I love the cover.
He knew about events that were long before his time because he loved reading history.
idioms.usageTip: Often used when introducing someone to something from the past that they wouldn't know about. Can also mean something happened too early in someone's career or life.

idioms.definition: For a very long time; used to emphasise how long something has lasted or how long it has been since something happened
examples: I haven't been to this part of town for donkey's years.
She's been working at that company for donkey's years.
I haven't seen him in donkey's years – we completely lost touch.
idioms.usageTip: Informal and slightly humorous. 'In donkey's years' and 'for donkey's years' are both used. Implies an exaggeratedly long time.

idioms.definition: Very rarely; almost never; used to describe something that happens very infrequently
examples: We only go out for a fancy meal once in a blue moon.
She visits her parents once in a blue moon, even though they live nearby.
Opportunities like this come along once in a blue moon, so don't miss it.
idioms.usageTip: A blue moon is a rare astronomical event. The idiom exaggerates rarity for effect. Often used to contrast something unusual with what is expected.

idioms.definition: Suddenly and unexpectedly; without any warning or prior indication
examples: Out of the blue, she received a job offer from a company she'd never applied to.
His resignation came completely out of the blue – no one saw it coming.
I was just walking home when, out of the blue, I bumped into an old friend.
idioms.usageTip: Describes something that happens without any warning. Often used with verbs like 'arrive', 'appear', 'happen', 'come'. Similar to 'out of nowhere'.

fillInExercises.sentence: You should fix that leak under the sink now. ___ – it'll only get worse if you ignore it.
options: A stitch in time saves nine
fillInExercises.correct: A stitch in time saves nine
fillInExercises.explanation: 'A stitch in time saves nine' means acting promptly prevents a small problem from becoming a bigger one.

fillInExercises.sentence: 'Are you going to tell me what the surprise is?' 'Don't worry, ___!'
fillInExercises.explanation: 'All in good time' is used to tell someone to be patient and that something will happen when the moment is right.

fillInExercises.sentence: That song was ___ – it came out twenty years before you were born.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Before your time' means something happened or existed before the period when someone was old enough to know about it.

fillInExercises.sentence: I haven't eaten at this restaurant ___ – not since we celebrated your graduation.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'For donkey's years' means for a very long time, used informally to emphasise how long it has been.

fillInExercises.sentence: We treat ourselves to a weekend away ___ – maybe once or twice a year at most.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Once in a blue moon' describes something that happens very rarely.

fillInExercises.sentence: The phone call came completely ___ – she hadn't heard from him in years.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Out of the blue' means suddenly and unexpectedly, with no prior warning.

lines.speaker: Marcus
lines.text: You'll never guess who called me [out of the blue|out of the blue] this morning – Danny Clarke from secondary school!

lines.speaker: Ella
lines.text: No way! I haven't heard that name [for donkey's years|for donkey's years]. How is he?

lines.speaker: Marcus
lines.text: Really well, apparently. He's organising a reunion. We only get together [once in a blue moon|once in a blue moon], so it'll be great.

lines.speaker: Ella
lines.text: Definitely go. These chances don't come around often. Did he mention Sarah? She was class captain – probably [before your time|before your time] since you joined in Year 10.

lines.speaker: Marcus
lines.text: He did, actually. She's coming. Danny also said he's been meaning to organise this for years – classic case of procrastinating when [a stitch in time saves nine|a stitch in time saves nine] would have done the trick.

lines.speaker: Ella
lines.text: Ha! Well, better late than never. When is it?

lines.speaker: Marcus
lines.text: He hasn't confirmed yet. Said the venue booking is in progress – [all in good time|all in good time], apparently.

lines.speaker: Ella
lines.text: Well, let me know. I'd love to see everyone again.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: I got an email [out of the blue|out of the blue] from a client we haven't worked with in ages.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Really? We haven't heard from them [in donkey's years|in donkey's years]. What do they want?

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: A full website redesign. It's the kind of project that comes along [once in a blue moon|once in a blue moon], so I want to jump at it.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: Agreed. Their old site has needed an update for years – [a stitch in time saves nine|a stitch in time saves nine], but they left it far too long.

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: Their current developer is someone [before my time|before your time] here – apparently he set everything up about fifteen years ago.

lines.speaker: Tom
lines.text: That explains the outdated code. Do you have a proposal ready?

lines.speaker: Nadia
lines.text: [All in good time|All in good time] – I only got the email an hour ago. Give me until tomorrow morning.

quizExercises.instructions: Match each idiom with its correct meaning.

pairs.meaning: dealing with a problem early prevents it becoming worse
something will happen eventually; no need to rush
existing or happening before you were born or old enough to remember

quizExercises.sentence: That band was ___ – they broke up a decade before you started school.
quizExercises.explanation: 'Before your time' means something existed or happened before you were old enough to know about it.

quizExercises.sentence: I know you're eager for an answer, but these decisions can't be rushed – ___.
quizExercises.explanation: 'All in good time' is used to reassure someone that the right moment will come – there is no need to hurry.

quizExercises.question: Which situation best illustrates 'out of the blue'?
options: A friend announces a party they have been planning for months
A company unexpectedly offers you a job without you having applied
A train arrives late after a well-publicised delay
quizExercises.explanation: 'Out of the blue' describes something that happens with no warning whatsoever, like an unexpected job offer.

quizExercises.question: Which behaviour is best described as happening 'once in a blue moon'?
options: Going for a walk every weekend
Taking an overseas holiday only every few years
quizExercises.explanation: 'Once in a blue moon' means something that happens very rarely, like an overseas holiday that only occurs every few years.
